1.synthesis: Paul commends to the Corinthians the example of the Macedonian churches — Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea — who gave with extraordinary liberality despite severe affliction and extreme poverty. Their giving was an 'overflowing of joy' combined with deep poverty, producing a 'wealth of generosity' (v.2). They gave beyond their means, Paul testifies, and of their own accord — begging earnestly for the favor of participating in the relief of the saints (vv.3–4). Before giving money, they first gave themselves to the Lord and to the apostles by the will of God (v.5). Accordingly Paul urges Titus to complete among the Corinthians this 'gracious work' that he had already begun; and appeals to them to abound in this grace also, as they abound in faith, speech, knowledge, earnestness, and love (vv.6–7).
1.historical_context: The collection for Jerusalem (the 'poor saints') was a major undertaking of Paul's third missionary journey, representing both practical relief and a symbolic act of Gentile-Jewish solidarity within the one body of Christ. Gill notes that the Macedonian churches 'received the Gospel at first in much affliction, as did the church at Thessalonica … and afterwards suffered much from their countrymen for the profession of it, by reproaches, persecutions, imprisonments, confiscation of goods.' Against this background, giving to others from their own poverty was a moral astonishment. The collection is described as a 'grace' (charis) throughout these chapters — the same word used for God's grace — suggesting that the human act of generous giving participates in and reflects the divine act of giving.
1.literary_structure: Chapters 8 and 9 form a unified composition on the collection. The opening movement (vv.1–7) is entirely about example and motivation: the Macedonians serve as the positive proof that affliction and poverty are not disqualifications from generous giving. The key logic-move is in v.5: they gave themselves first, then gave their money. Self-donation precedes monetary donation; the person who has given himself to the Lord gives money as the natural overflow of that prior commitment. Chrysostom identifies Paul's strategy in v.7: he does not say 'that ye give' but 'that ye abound' — escalating the goal from contribution to excellence, and grounding it in the other excellences already present in the Corinthian church.
1.theological_application: Gill on v.1 identifies the 'grace of God' bestowed on the Macedonian churches as the source-explanation of their generosity: the ability to give such 'liberal benefactions to the churches, and towards the relief of the poor saints,' is itself 'the grace of God'; they were not naturally disposed to give so greatly but were enabled by divine grace. The 'grace of God' here is not merely enabling power but a specific gift of liberality — the same root-concept as the spiritual gift of giving in Romans 12:8. Gill on v.2 catches the paradox: 'for persons in prosperity, when all things go well with them, to be liberal, is not so wonderful; but for persons in poverty, in deep poverty, under trying afflictions, to abound in liberality, is something very surprising and extraordinary, and is entirely owing to the grace of God.' The Macedonians' poverty was not a disqualification but the very context in which the miracle of grace-enabled giving occurred.
1.christology: 'They gave themselves first to the Lord' (v.5) is the foundational christological act underlying all Christian generosity. The Lord to whom they gave themselves is Christ — whose own self-giving ('though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor,' v.9) is the archetype and motivation for all human self-giving. The collection is not merely philanthropic; it is participatory in the pattern of the incarnation. Just as Christ gave himself before he gave blessings, so the Macedonians gave themselves before they gave money — and the priority of the first-gift makes the second-gift coherent and overflowing.

8.synthesis: Paul does not command but tests the genuineness of the Corinthians' love by the example of others' earnestness (v.8). The supreme appeal is christological: 'You know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ: though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (v.9). He advises rather than commands: the Corinthians had begun a year earlier, and now they should complete the work, so that readiness of desire matches completion of result (vv.10–11). Acceptability depends on what a person has, not what they don't have (v.12). The aim is not equality through impoverishing the givers but through an equalization of needs: at this time their abundance supplying the others' needs, with the prospect of reciprocation (vv.13–14). The precedent is manna: the one who gathered much had nothing over, and the one who gathered little had no lack (v.15).
8.historical_context: Gill notes carefully that Paul 'did not speak in an imperious manner, extorting from them a collection, or laying his apostolical injunctions upon them to make one; he did not go about to force or oblige them to it, for men in such cases must act willingly, and what they do, must do of their own accord.' The refusal to command is itself theologically significant: coerced generosity defeats the point. The manna precedent in v.15 draws on Exodus 16:18, where the miraculous provision of manna demonstrated that the Lord's economy runs not on hoarding but on sufficiency-for-all — a pattern Paul applies to the distribution of material resources within the body of Christ.
8.literary_structure: The christological appeal of v.9 is the theological center of the entire section. The movement is: argument from testing genuineness (v.8) → supreme christological example (v.9) → practical advice (vv.10–11) → governing principle of proportionate giving (v.12) → equalization logic (vv.13–14) → scriptural precedent (v.15). The christological example is placed before the practical advice precisely because it is the motivational ground; the manna precedent at the close shows that the divine economy has always operated this way. The section refuses both compulsion and minimalism: Paul neither commands nor sets a floor; he points to Christ and trusts the logic to do its work.
8.theological_application: Gill on v.9 calls Christ's poverty 'a very forcible argument to engage to liberality': the wonder of the incarnation — the eternal Son voluntarily entering poverty so that the poor might be enriched — is 'well known to all them that have truly believed in Christ; of this they are not and cannot be ignorant, his love, good will, and favour are so manifest.' The theological argument is: those who have been enriched by Christ's poverty (justification, adoption, the Spirit, eternal life) cannot refuse to share their temporal resources with those in need. The 'richness' believers have received is incomparably greater than any money they might give; the 'poverty' Christ entered was incomparably more costly than the poverty of the Macedonians. The calculus of the incarnation makes Christian stinginess not only uncharitable but logically incoherent. Matthew Henry sees the manna text as proof of God's own principle of equalization: 'God so ordered it by a miracle, to show his will that there should be an equality in such things; and this was typified by the manna.'
8.christology: 'Though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (v.9) is one of the most compressed statements of the incarnation in the Pauline letters. The 'richness' refers to his eternal preexistence in the divine glory; the 'poverty' to the full range of the incarnate condition — not only material poverty but the limitation, weakness, and ultimately the cross. The phrase 'for your sake' (di' hymas) makes the christological descent explicitly soteriological: the purpose of the poverty is the enrichment of the recipients. This participatory enrichment — we become rich by his poverty — is the pattern that grounds the entire collection argument: as Christ's giving produced abundance for others, so the givers' current abundance is the instrument by which the lacking are supplied.

16.synthesis: Paul commends the messengers who will carry the collection: Titus, who has the same earnest care for the Corinthians that Paul himself has (v.16); an unnamed brother who is famous for his gospel service throughout the churches and has been appointed by the churches to travel with the gift (vv.18–19); and a third brother proved in many matters and earnest (v.22). Paul's motive in sending multiple trusted messengers is explicit: he wants to avoid any accusation about the handling of this generous gift, 'for we aim at what is honorable not only in the Lord's sight but also in the sight of man' (vv.20–21). He closes by commending them as the glory of Christ and the proof of Paul's boasting about the Corinthians (vv.23–24).
16.historical_context: The sending of multiple named witnesses to accompany financial gifts was standard practice in the ancient church to protect against accusation of embezzlement. Paul was aware that his collection had already attracted suspicion from his opponents, and the arrangement of plural accountable messengers was both a moral and a strategic precaution. Chrysostom observes that Paul's detailed commendation of Titus and the brothers served two functions: it honored the messengers and encouraged the Corinthians to give with fuller confidence: 'he that feels confidence as to him that ministereth, and suspects not those who are to be receivers, gives with the fuller bountifulness.'
16.literary_structure: The commendation section follows a three-part pattern: Titus (vv.16–17) → the famous brother appointed by the churches (vv.18–19) → the proved brother (v.22). Each commendation emphasizes a different credential: Titus has earnest personal care; the famous brother has public apostolic recognition; the proved brother has tested character. The arrangement signals that the collection is not a private apostolic project but a church-wide enterprise, with representatives from multiple communities who together guarantee its integrity. The close appeal to 'show them before the churches the proof of your love' (v.24) frames the collection as a public demonstration of character, not merely a private transaction.
16.theological_application: Chrysostom focuses on Paul's praise of Titus as a pastoral strategy: 'Again he praises Titus. For since he had discoursed of almsgiving, he afterwards discourseth also of those who are to receive the money from them and carry it away. For this was of aid towards this collection, and towards increasing the forwardness of the contributors.' The practical wisdom here is considerable: people give more freely when they trust the administrators. But Paul's care for 'what is honorable not only in the Lord's sight but also in the sight of man' (v.21) goes beyond strategic calculation — it reflects the conviction that Christian financial stewardship should be above reproach as a matter of witness. Gill reads this as Paul's consistent practice: avoiding every appearance of covetousness or self-enrichment, so that the gospel itself would not be brought into disrepute.
16.christology: The messengers are called 'apostles of the churches and the glory of Christ' (v.23). The phrase 'glory of Christ' (doxa Christou) is striking: they manifest and display Christ's character and purposes through their service. In carrying the collection — itself an act of love motivated by Christ's own self-giving — they become visible expressions of his glory in the world. This connects the mundane task of financial administration to the cosmic purpose of displaying Christ; the collection is not merely charitable logistics but an act of christological witness in which the churches demonstrate that Christ's love has genuinely transformed their relationship to possessions and to one another.
